report:
## Revenue Growth

Over the FY2022–FY2025 period, Eli Lilly has demonstrated dramatically faster revenue growth than Merck. LLY grew from $28.5B in FY2022 [LLY:Q0] to $65.2B in FY2025 [LLY:Q3], representing roughly 128% cumulative growth and a compound annual growth rate well above 30%, fueled primarily by its GLP-1/incretin franchise. Merck, by contrast, grew from $59.3B in FY2022 [MRK:Q0] to $65.0B in FY2025 [MRK:Q3], a cumulative gain of roughly 10% over the same period, reflecting a much more mature, slower-growing revenue base. Notably, LLY's FY2025 revenues of $65.2B [LLY:Q3] have now essentially caught up to and slightly surpassed MRK's $65.0B [MRK:Q3] — a remarkable convergence given that Merck was more than twice Lilly's size just three years earlier.

**MRK:** Merck has delivered steady but modest top-line growth, moving from $59.3B in FY2022 [MRK:Q0] to $65.0B in FY2025 [MRK:Q3], a cumulative increase of roughly 10% over three years. Year-over-year gains have been incremental — revenues rose from $60.1B in FY2023 [MRK:Q1] to $64.2B in FY2024 [MRK:Q2] and then to $65.0B in FY2025 [MRK:Q3] — suggesting a large, diversified business growing broadly in line with the pharmaceutical industry average rather than experiencing a step-change acceleration.

**LLY:** Eli Lilly has been one of the fastest-growing large-cap pharmaceutical companies in recent history, with revenues nearly doubling from $28.5B in FY2022 [LLY:Q0] to $45.0B in FY2024 [LLY:Q2] and then surging to $65.2B in FY2025 [LLY:Q3]. The acceleration is particularly striking in the most recent year, where revenues jumped roughly 45% year-over-year from FY2024 to FY2025 [LLY:Q2, LLY:Q3], driven by explosive demand for its GLP-1 and diabetes/obesity portfolio.

---

quality.notes: The report is exceptionally clear and well-structured, with a strong comparative narrative and consistent citation of source tags. The convergence insight (LLY catching MRK in absolute revenue) is a compelling analytical highlight. Minor deduction on honesty/uncertainty: the report states figures with high precision and confidence (e.g., '~128% cumulative growth,' '~45% year-over-year') without any hedging about data quality, restatements, or the fact that FY2025 figures may be trailing-twelve-month estimates rather than closed fiscal years. A brief acknowledgment of data limitations or estimation methodology would strengthen epistemic honesty.
